main_paper.raw: {"methods":["OPTIMA framework: iterative generate, rank, select, and train paradigm for LLM-based multi-agent systems","Reward function balancing task performance, token efficiency, and communication readability","Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for MAS training","Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for MAS training","Hybrid approaches combining SFT and DPO","Integration of Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)-inspired techniques for DPO data generation, conceptualizing conversation turns as tree nodes"],"problems":["Low communication efficiency in LLM-based multi-agent systems","Poor scalability of LLM-based MAS","Lack of effective parameter-updating optimization methods for multi-agent collaboration","Inefficient inter-agent communication leading to high token usage and computational cost","Lack of methods to optimize LLM-based MAS as a unified system"],"datasets":["Information-asymmetric question answering tasks (referencing Chen et al., 2024d; Liu et al., 2024)","Debate tasks including mathematical and reasoning tasks (referencing Du et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2024b; Wu et al., 2023)"],"metrics":["Task performance","Token efficiency (token usage)","Communication interpretability/readability"],"results":[{"metric":"Task performance improvement over single-agent and vanilla MAS baselines","value":"up to 2.8x increase"},{"metric":"Token usage reduction","value":"up to 90% reduction (less than 10% tokens used compared to baseline)"}],"novelty_claims":["OPTIMA is a novel framework that simultaneously optimizes both communication efficiency and task effectiveness in LLM-based multi-agent systems.","Introduction of an iterative generate, rank, select, and train paradigm with a reward function balancing multiple objectives.","First to integrate MCTS-inspired techniques for DPO data generation in multi-agent LLM training, conceptualizing conversation turns as tree nodes.","Provides a unified parameter-updating method for LLM-based MAS, addressing both efficiency and collective optimization.","Demonstrates substantial improvements in both token efficiency and task performance across diverse multi-agent tasks."]}

methods: OPTIMA framework: iterative generate, rank, select, and train paradigm for LLM-based multi-agent systems
Reward function balancing task performance, token efficiency, and communication readability
Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for MAS training
Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for MAS training
Hybrid approaches combining SFT and DPO
Integration of Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)-inspired techniques for DPO data generation, conceptualizing conversation turns as tree nodes
problems: Low communication efficiency in LLM-based multi-agent systems
Poor scalability of LLM-based MAS
Lack of effective parameter-updating optimization methods for multi-agent collaboration
Inefficient inter-agent communication leading to high token usage and computational cost
Lack of methods to optimize LLM-based MAS as a unified system
datasets: Information-asymmetric question answering tasks (referencing Chen et al., 2024d; Liu et al., 2024)
Debate tasks including mathematical and reasoning tasks (referencing Du et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2024b; Wu et al., 2023)

results.metric: Task performance improvement over single-agent and vanilla MAS baselines

results.value: up to 90% reduction (less than 10% tokens used compared to baseline)

novelty_claims: OPTIMA is a novel framework that simultaneously optimizes both communication efficiency and task effectiveness in LLM-based multi-agent systems.
Introduction of an iterative generate, rank, select, and train paradigm with a reward function balancing multiple objectives.
First to integrate MCTS-inspired techniques for DPO data generation in multi-agent LLM training, conceptualizing conversation turns as tree nodes.
Provides a unified parameter-updating method for LLM-based MAS, addressing both efficiency and collective optimization.
Demonstrates substantial improvements in both token efficiency and task performance across diverse multi-agent tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["CoELA: Cooperative Embodied Language Agent, a cognitive-inspired modular framework integrating perception, memory, communication, planning, and execution modules, leveraging LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, LLAMA-2) for planning and communication","Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) of open LMs (e.g., LLAMA-2) to improve performance in multi-agent cooperation tasks"],"problems":["Multi-agent cooperation with decentralized control and partial observation","Efficient communication under costly communication constraints","Long-horizon, multi-objective task completion in embodied environments","Building embodied agents that can cooperate with both other agents and humans"],"datasets":["ThreeDWorld Multi-Agent Transport (TDW-MAT)","Communicative Watch-And-Help (C-WAH)"],"metrics":["Task efficiency (e.g., percentage improvement over baselines)","User trust in human-agent cooperation (from user study)"],"results":[{"metric":"Task efficiency improvement over strong planning-based baselines (using GPT-4)","value":">40%"},{"metric":"User trust in human-agent cooperation (qualitative, from user study)","value":"CoELA communicating in natural language earns more trust and cooperates more effectively with humans"}],"novelty_claims":["First to leverage LLMs in a modular cognitive-inspired framework for decentralized multi-agent embodied cooperation with costly communication and partial observation","Demonstrates emergent efficient communication and planning in multi-agent embodied tasks using LLMs (GPT-4)","Shows that fine-tuned open LMs (e.g., LLAMA-2 with LoRA) can achieve promising performance in these tasks","Presents a user study showing that natural language communication by agents increases human trust and cooperation"]}

methods: CoELA: Cooperative Embodied Language Agent, a cognitive-inspired modular framework integrating perception, memory, communication, planning, and execution modules, leveraging LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, LLAMA-2) for planning and communication
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) of open LMs (e.g., LLAMA-2) to improve performance in multi-agent cooperation tasks
problems: Multi-agent cooperation with decentralized control and partial observation
Efficient communication under costly communication constraints
Long-horizon, multi-objective task completion in embodied environments
Building embodied agents that can cooperate with both other agents and humans
metrics: Task efficiency (e.g., percentage improvement over baselines)
User trust in human-agent cooperation (from user study)

results.metric: Task efficiency improvement over strong planning-based baselines (using GPT-4)
User trust in human-agent cooperation (qualitative, from user study)
results.value: CoELA communicating in natural language earns more trust and cooperates more effectively with humans

novelty_claims: First to leverage LLMs in a modular cognitive-inspired framework for decentralized multi-agent embodied cooperation with costly communication and partial observation
Demonstrates emergent efficient communication and planning in multi-agent embodied tasks using LLMs (GPT-4)
Shows that fine-tuned open LMs (e.g., LLAMA-2 with LoRA) can achieve promising performance in these tasks
Presents a user study showing that natural language communication by agents increases human trust and cooperation

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Reflexion framework (verbal reinforcement learning via linguistic feedback and episodic memory)","Conversion of environment feedback (binary/scalar) into verbal/textual summaries","Storing reflective feedback in episodic memory buffer for future decision-making","Three feedback incorporation methods: binary environment feedback, pre-defined heuristics, self-evaluation (e.g., LLM-based classification, self-written unit tests)"],"problems":["Inefficiency of traditional reinforcement learning for language agents due to high sample and compute requirements","Difficulty for language agents to learn efficiently from trial-and-error without expensive model fine-tuning","Lack of effective mechanisms for language agents to learn from prior mistakes in a lightweight, interpretable way"],"datasets":["AlfWorld (decision-making tasks)","HotPotQA (reasoning tasks)","HumanEval (Python programming tasks)","LeetcodeHardGym (newly introduced, 40 hard Leetcode questions in 19 languages)"],"metrics":["pass@1 accuracy (HumanEval)","Absolute improvement in task success rate (AlfWorld, HotPotQA, HumanEval)"],"results":[{"metric":"pass@1 accuracy (HumanEval)","value":"91%"},{"metric":"pass@1 accuracy (HumanEval, GPT-4 baseline)","value":"80%"},{"metric":"Absolute improvement in AlfWorld (decision-making)","value":"22% in 12 iterative learning steps"},{"metric":"Absolute improvement in HotPotQA (reasoning)","value":"20%"},{"metric":"Absolute improvement in HumanEval (programming)","value":"11%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Reflexion, a new paradigm for verbal reinforcement learning that does not require model fine-tuning but instead uses linguistic feedback and episodic memory.","Demonstrates that self-reflection in LLMs can be leveraged for efficient learning from prior mistakes across diverse tasks.","Introduces LeetcodeHardGym, a new code-generation RL gym environment with hard Leetcode questions in multiple languages.","Shows that Reflexion achieves state-of-the-art results on code generation benchmarks and significant improvements over strong baselines in decision-making, reasoning, and programming tasks."]}

methods: Reflexion framework (verbal reinforcement learning via linguistic feedback and episodic memory)
Conversion of environment feedback (binary/scalar) into verbal/textual summaries
Storing reflective feedback in episodic memory buffer for future decision-making
Three feedback incorporation methods: binary environment feedback, pre-defined heuristics, self-evaluation (e.g., LLM-based classification, self-written unit tests)
problems: Inefficiency of traditional reinforcement learning for language agents due to high sample and compute requirements
Difficulty for language agents to learn efficiently from trial-and-error without expensive model fine-tuning
Lack of effective mechanisms for language agents to learn from prior mistakes in a lightweight, interpretable way
datasets: LeetcodeHardGym (newly introduced, 40 hard Leetcode questions in 19 languages)
metrics: Absolute improvement in task success rate (AlfWorld, HotPotQA, HumanEval)
novelty_claims: Proposes Reflexion, a new paradigm for verbal reinforcement learning that does not require model fine-tuning but instead uses linguistic feedback and episodic memory.
Demonstrates that self-reflection in LLMs can be leveraged for efficient learning from prior mistakes across diverse tasks.
Introduces LeetcodeHardGym, a new code-generation RL gym environment with hard Leetcode questions in multiple languages.
Shows that Reflexion achieves state-of-the-art results on code generation benchmarks and significant improvements over strong baselines in decision-making, reasoning, and programming tasks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) methodology: a layered architecture where each layer consists of multiple LLM agents, each taking outputs from previous layer agents as auxiliary information for response generation","Iterative refinement of responses through multiple MoA layers","Selection of LLMs for each layer based on performance metrics and diversity of outputs"],"problems":["How to harness the collective expertise of multiple LLMs to improve language model capabilities","Overcoming inherent constraints of individual LLMs such as model size and training data limitations","Improving response quality and robustness of LLMs without costly retraining or scaling"],"datasets":["AlpacaEval 2.0","MT-Bench","FLASK"],"metrics":["Win rate (e.g., LC win rate on AlpacaEval 2.0)"],"results":[{"metric":"AlpacaEval 2.0 win rate (MoA, open-source LLMs)","value":"65.1%"},{"metric":"AlpacaEval 2.0 win rate (GPT-4 Omni)","value":"57.5%"},{"metric":"AlpacaEval 2.0 win rate (MoA, SOTA)","value":"65.8%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) framework that leverages multiple LLMs in a layered, collaborative architecture to enhance response quality.","Identifies and demonstrates the 'collaborativeness' phenomenon among LLMs, where models improve when given outputs from other models, even if those are of lower quality.","Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple competitive benchmarks (AlpacaEval 2.0, MT-Bench, FLASK) surpassing previous best models including GPT-4 Omni."]}

methods: Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) methodology: a layered architecture where each layer consists of multiple LLM agents, each taking outputs from previous layer agents as auxiliary information for response generation
Iterative refinement of responses through multiple MoA layers
Selection of LLMs for each layer based on performance metrics and diversity of outputs
problems: How to harness the collective expertise of multiple LLMs to improve language model capabilities
Overcoming inherent constraints of individual LLMs such as model size and training data limitations
Improving response quality and robustness of LLMs without costly retraining or scaling
metrics: Win rate (e.g., LC win rate on AlpacaEval 2.0)
novelty_claims: Proposes a novel Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) framework that leverages multiple LLMs in a layered, collaborative architecture to enhance response quality.
Identifies and demonstrates the 'collaborativeness' phenomenon among LLMs, where models improve when given outputs from other models, even if those are of lower quality.
Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple competitive benchmarks (AlpacaEval 2.0, MT-Bench, FLASK) surpassing previous best models including GPT-4 Omni.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Exploration-based Trajectory Optimization (ETO): an iterative learning algorithm that collects failure trajectories during agent exploration and refines the policy failure-success trajectory pairs"],"problems":["Improving the performance of open-source LLM agents in complex interactive tasks","Overcoming the limitations of imitation learning methods that rely solely on expert trajectories, which can lead to sub-optimal policies and poor generalization","Enabling LLM agents to learn effectively from exploration failures, not just successes","Enhancing generalization and efficiency of LLM agents, especially in out-of-distribution and low-expert-data scenarios"],"datasets":["WebShop (for web navigation)","ScienceWorld (for simulated science experiments)","ALFWorld (for embodied household tasks)"],"metrics":["Task success rate","Out-of-distribution test performance (e.g., success rate)","Task-solving efficiency (e.g., reward per action, number of action steps)"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance improvement over SFT on ScienceWorld out-of-distribution test set","value":"22% increase"},{"metric":"Performance on three complex tasks (WebShop, ScienceWorld, ALFWorld)","value":"ETO consistently exceeds SFT and other baselines by a large margin (exact numbers not specified in provided text)"},{"metric":"Task-solving efficiency","value":"ETO achieves higher rewards with fewer action steps (quantitative details not specified in provided text)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes ETO, a novel learning approach for LLM agents that leverages exploration failures, not just expert successes, to improve policy learning","First to demonstrate that learning from contrastive failure-success trajectory pairs via DPO loss can significantly enhance open LLM agent performance","Shows that ETO enables strong generalization to out-of-distribution tasks and maintains effectiveness even without expert trajectories (self-play mode)"]}

methods: Exploration-based Trajectory Optimization (ETO): an iterative learning algorithm that collects failure trajectories during agent exploration and refines the policy via contrastive learning using trajectory preference pairs and DPO loss
Behavioral Cloning (BC) with Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) as a base agent construction method
Contrastive learning using failure-success trajectory pairs
problems: Improving the performance of open-source LLM agents in complex interactive tasks
Overcoming the limitations of imitation learning methods that rely solely on expert trajectories, which can lead to sub-optimal policies and poor generalization
Enabling LLM agents to learn effectively from exploration failures, not just successes
Enhancing generalization and efficiency of LLM agents, especially in out-of-distribution and low-expert-data scenarios
metrics: Task-solving efficiency (e.g., reward per action, number of action steps)

results.metric: Performance improvement over SFT on ScienceWorld out-of-distribution test set
Performance on three complex tasks (WebShop, ScienceWorld, ALFWorld)
results.value: ETO consistently exceeds SFT and other baselines by a large margin (exact numbers not specified in provided text)
ETO achieves higher rewards with fewer action steps (quantitative details not specified in provided text)

novelty_claims: Proposes ETO, a novel learning approach for LLM agents that leverages exploration failures, not just expert successes, to improve policy learning
First to demonstrate that learning from contrastive failure-success trajectory pairs via DPO loss can significantly enhance open LLM agent performance
Shows that ETO enables strong generalization to out-of-distribution tasks and maintains effectiveness even without expert trajectories (self-play mode)

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SimPO (Simple Preference Optimization): uses average log probability of a sequence as implicit reward, eliminates need for a reference model, and introduces a target reward margin to the Bradley-Terry objective"],"problems":["Improving offline preference optimization for large language models (LLMs)","Reducing complexity and compute/memory requirements in RLHF algorithms","Aligning reward functions more closely with generation metrics in preference optimization","Minimizing response length exploitation in preference optimization"],"datasets":["AlpacaEval 2","MT-Bench","Arena-Hard","Chatbot Arena (with real user votes)"],"metrics":["Length-controlled win rate (LC)","Raw win rate (WR)","Response length"],"results":[{"metric":"AlpacaEval 2 Length-controlled win rate (Gemma-2-9B-it-SimPO)","value":"72.4%"},{"metric":"AlpacaEval 2 Raw win rate (Gemma-2-9B-it-SimPO)","value":"65.9%"},{"metric":"Arena-Hard win rate (Gemma-2-9B-it-SimPO)","value":"59.1%"},{"metric":"Improvement over DPO on AlpacaEval 2","value":"up to 6.4 points"},{"metric":"Improvement over DPO on Arena-Hard","value":"up to 7.5 points"},{"metric":"Chatbot Arena rank (<10B models, Gemma-2-9B-it-SimPO)","value":"1st"}],"novelty_claims":["SimPO uses the average log probability of a sequence as an implicit reward, directly aligning the reward with the generation metric and eliminating the need for a reference model.","SimPO introduces a target reward margin to the Bradley-Terry objective, encouraging a larger margin between winning and losing responses.","SimPO is more compute and memory efficient than DPO and other reference-based methods due to its reference-free design.","SimPO achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models under 10B parameters, outperforming DPO and other recent methods."]}

methods: SimPO (Simple Preference Optimization): uses average log probability of a sequence as implicit reward, eliminates need for a reference model, and introduces a target reward margin to the Bradley-Terry objective
problems: Improving offline preference optimization for large language models (LLMs)
Reducing complexity and compute/memory requirements in RLHF algorithms
Aligning reward functions more closely with generation metrics in preference optimization
Minimizing response length exploitation in preference optimization
novelty_claims: SimPO uses the average log probability of a sequence as an implicit reward, directly aligning the reward with the generation metric and eliminating the need for a reference model.
SimPO introduces a target reward margin to the Bradley-Terry objective, encouraging a larger margin between winning and losing responses.
SimPO is more compute and memory efficient than DPO and other reference-based methods due to its reference-free design.
SimPO achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models under 10B parameters, outperforming DPO and other recent methods.

methods: Multi-agent debate (MAD) with sparse communication topology (neighbor-connected topology)
Extension of MAD to multimodal reasoning tasks
Extension of MAD to alignment labeling tasks
Assignment of stronger LLMs to agents with higher centrality in non-regular graph settings
problems: High computational cost of fully-connected multi-agent debate frameworks
Effect of communication connectivity (sparsity) on the effectiveness and efficiency of multi-agent debate
Application of multi-agent debate to multimodal reasoning and alignment labeling tasks

results.metric: Average input token cost (reasoning tasks, neighbor-connected vs fully-connected)

novelty_claims: Systematic investigation of the effect of sparse communication topology in multi-agent debate frameworks for LLMs.
Demonstration that sparse communication can achieve comparable or superior performance to fully-connected debate while significantly reducing computational costs.
Extension of the multi-agent debate framework to multimodal reasoning and alignment labeling tasks.
Empirical finding that assigning stronger LLMs to agents with higher centrality in non-regular graph settings yields better performance.
Provision of insights into why sparsity in communication enhances the efficiency and effectiveness of multi-agent debate.

Iterative Agent Optimization with step-level and outcome-level DPO, and SFT losses

problems: Lack of process supervision signals in LLM agent training

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Self-Organized multi-Agent framework (SoA) for code generation and optimization","Automatic multiplication of agents based on problem complexity","Independent and collaborative code generation and modification by multiple agents"],"problems":["Scalability limitations of single-agent LLM-based code generation for large and complex codebases","Context length constraints in LLMs that hinder efficient generation and modification of large-scale code","Inefficiency of single-agent approaches in managing and generating code for complex software projects"],"datasets":["HumanEval"],"metrics":["Pass@1 accuracy","Code volume generated","Code volume handled per agent"],"results":[{"metric":"Pass@1 accuracy","value":"SoA surpasses single-agent baseline by 5%"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a self-organized multi-agent framework (SoA) for scalable and efficient large-scale code generation and optimization","Automatic dynamic multiplication of agents based on problem complexity, enabling indefinite scaling of code volume","Each agent manages a constant amount of code regardless of overall codebase size, overcoming LLM context length limitations","Demonstrated superior performance over strong single-agent baselines on HumanEval benchmark"]}

Automatic multiplication of agents based on problem complexity
Independent and collaborative code generation and modification by multiple agents
problems: Scalability limitations of single-agent LLM-based code generation for large and complex codebases
Context length constraints in LLMs that hinder efficient generation and modification of large-scale code
Inefficiency of single-agent approaches in managing and generating code for complex software projects
novelty_claims: Introduction of a self-organized multi-agent framework (SoA) for scalable and efficient large-scale code generation and optimization
Automatic dynamic multiplication of agents based on problem complexity, enabling indefinite scaling of code volume
Each agent manages a constant amount of code regardless of overall codebase size, overcoming LLM context length limitations
Demonstrated superior performance over strong single-agent baselines on HumanEval benchmark

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["iAgents (Informative Multi-Agent Systems) framework","InfoNav (novel agent reasoning mechanism for information navigation)","Mixed memory mechanism for accurate and comprehensive information exchange"],"problems":["Collaborative task solving by autonomous agents under information asymmetry, where each agent only has access to its human user's information","Enabling effective agent communication and collaboration without centralized information sharing due to privacy and dynamic information constraints"],"datasets":["InformativeBench (the first benchmark for evaluating LLM agents' task-solving ability under information asymmetry)"],"metrics":["Accuracy (on InformativeBench)","Task completion time","Number of dialogue turns","Scale of social network (number of individuals and relationships handled)"],"results":[{"metric":"Average accuracy on InformativeBench","value":"50.48%"},{"metric":"Accuracy on most challenging InformativeBench task","value":"22.8%"},{"metric":"Scale of social network handled","value":"140 individuals, 588 relationships"},{"metric":"Number of dialogue turns","value":"over 30"},{"metric":"Number of messages retrieved","value":"nearly 70,000"},{"metric":"Task completion time","value":"within 3 minutes"}],"novelty_claims":["First to address information asymmetry in multi-agent systems for human collaboration, shifting the research perspective from holistic system to individual agents.","Proposes iAgents, a new MAS paradigm that mirrors human social networks and enables proactive information exchange to overcome information asymmetry.","Introduces InfoNav, a novel agent reasoning mechanism for navigating communication under information asymmetry.","Develops a mixed memory mechanism for accurate and comprehensive information exchange among agents.","Presents InformativeBench, the first benchmark specifically designed to evaluate multi-agent collaboration under information asymmetry."]}

methods: iAgents (Informative Multi-Agent Systems) framework
InfoNav (novel agent reasoning mechanism for information navigation)
Mixed memory mechanism for accurate and comprehensive information exchange
problems: Collaborative task solving by autonomous agents under information asymmetry, where each agent only has access to its human user's information
Enabling effective agent communication and collaboration without centralized information sharing due to privacy and dynamic information constraints
datasets: InformativeBench (the first benchmark for evaluating LLM agents' task-solving ability under information asymmetry)

novelty_claims: First to address information asymmetry in multi-agent systems for human collaboration, shifting the research perspective from holistic system to individual agents.
Proposes iAgents, a new MAS paradigm that mirrors human social networks and enables proactive information exchange to overcome information asymmetry.
Introduces InfoNav, a novel agent reasoning mechanism for navigating communication under information asymmetry.
Develops a mixed memory mechanism for accurate and comprehensive information exchange among agents.
Presents InformativeBench, the first benchmark specifically designed to evaluate multi-agent collaboration under information asymmetry.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) framework: multiple LLM agents debate in a 'tit for tat' manner with a judge managing the process","Adaptive break strategy in the debate process","Analysis of 'tit for tat' state levels in agent debate"],"problems":["Degeneration-of-Thought (DoT) problem in self-reflection for LLMs: LLMs become overconfident and unable to generate novel thoughts after initial answers, even if incorrect","LLMs' lack of divergent thinking and difficulty in complex reasoning tasks"],"datasets":["Commonsense Machine Translation (Common MT)","Counter-Intuitive Arithmetic Reasoning (Counter-Intuitive AR)"],"metrics":["Task accuracy (on Common MT and Counter-Intuitive AR)","Disagreement rate between debate/self-reflection iterations"],"results":[{"metric":"Task accuracy (GPT-3.5-Turbo with MAD vs. GPT-4 on Common MT)","value":"GPT-3.5-Turbo with MAD surpasses GPT-4 performance on Common MT"},{"metric":"Disagreement rate (self-reflection vs. debate)","value":"Self-reflection shows low disagreement, indicating less divergent thinking; debate maintains higher disagreement, indicating more exploration of alternatives"}],"novelty_claims":["First to propose and define the Degeneration-of-Thought (DoT) problem in LLM self-reflection","Propose the Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) framework to address DoT and encourage divergent thinking in LLMs","Show that MAD enables GPT-3.5-Turbo to outperform GPT-4 on a challenging commonsense reasoning task","Provide analysis on the importance of adaptive break and 'tit for tat' state in multi-agent debate for LLMs","Identify that LLM-based judges may be biased towards agents using the same LLM backbone"]}

methods: Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) framework: multiple LLM agents debate in a 'tit for tat' manner with a judge managing the process
Adaptive break strategy in the debate process
Analysis of 'tit for tat' state levels in agent debate
problems: Degeneration-of-Thought (DoT) problem in self-reflection for LLMs: LLMs become overconfident and unable to generate novel thoughts after initial answers, even if incorrect
LLMs' lack of divergent thinking and difficulty in complex reasoning tasks
metrics: Task accuracy (on Common MT and Counter-Intuitive AR)

results.metric: Task accuracy (GPT-3.5-Turbo with MAD vs. GPT-4 on Common MT)
results.value: GPT-3.5-Turbo with MAD surpasses GPT-4 performance on Common MT
Self-reflection shows low disagreement, indicating less divergent thinking; debate maintains higher disagreement, indicating more exploration of alternatives

novelty_claims: First to propose and define the Degeneration-of-Thought (DoT) problem in LLM self-reflection
Propose the Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) framework to address DoT and encourage divergent thinking in LLMs
Show that MAD enables GPT-3.5-Turbo to outperform GPT-4 on a challenging commonsense reasoning task
Provide analysis on the importance of adaptive break and 'tit for tat' state in multi-agent debate for LLMs
Identify that LLM-based judges may be biased towards agents using the same LLM backbone

improvement","value":"3.3% to 5.7% increase for different LLMs"},{"metric":"Token usage reduction in multi-agent communication","value":"up to 72.7% reduction"},{"metric":"Communicative effectiveness","value":"Maintained (no loss in performance despite reduced token usage)"}],"novelty_claims":["First comprehensive study challenging the default use of NL in LLM reasoning and methods: Prompting LLMs to autonomously select the most suitable format (NL or non-NL) before reasoning or communicating
Adding instructions to Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompts to encourage exploration of non-NL formats
Allowing LLMs to devise a format from limited task instructions/examples
Transferring devised formats across different LLMs
Using structured communication formats (e.g., logical expressions, ordered lists, markdown tables) in multi-agent communication
problems: Determining whether natural language (NL) is the optimal format for LLM reasoning and multi-agent communication
Improving reasoning efficiency and communicative efficiency of LLMs by leveraging alternative (non-NL) formats
Reducing token usage in multi-agent communication without sacrificing effectiveness
Exploring the transferability of devised communication formats across different LLMs
metrics: Reasoning efficiency (performance improvement in reasoning tasks)
Communicative effectiveness (maintaining performance while reducing tokens)

results.metric: Token usage reduction in multi-agent communication

results.value: Maintained (no loss in performance despite reduced token usage)

novelty_claims: First comprehensive study challenging the default use of NL in LLM reasoning and communication by systematically exploring non-NL formats.
Demonstrates that LLMs can autonomously select and devise suitable non-NL formats for reasoning and communication, leading to measurable improvements.
Shows that devised formats are transferable across different LLMs.
Finds that LLM-devised structured communication formats naturally resemble established agent communication languages, suggesting a natural evolution towards efficient, structured agent communication.
Empirically demonstrates significant reduction in token usage and improved efficiency without sacrificing communicative effectiveness.

methods: Solo Performance Prompting (SPP): prompting a single LLM to dynamically identify, simulate, and collaborate with multiple fine-grained personas in a multi-turn, self-collaborative process
Comparative evaluation with fixed-persona variants (e.g., Camel, ExpertPrompting) and standard prompting baselines
problems: Factual hallucination in large language models (LLMs) on knowledge-intensive tasks
Lack of slow-thinking and collaborative cognitive processes in LLMs for complex reasoning tasks
Limitations of previous prompting methods (e.g., fixed personas, need for multiple LLMs, lack of dynamic persona identification)

results.value: SPP reduces factual hallucination compared to standard prompting and Chain-of-Thought methods (exact quantitative values not provided in the excerpt)
SPP maintains or improves reasoning performance compared to baselines (exact quantitative values not provided in the excerpt)
Cognitive synergy emerges in GPT-4 with SPP, but not in GPT-3.5-turbo or Llama2-13b-chat

novelty_claims: Introduction of Solo Performance Prompting (SPP), a method that enables a single LLM to dynamically identify and simulate multiple fine-grained personas for self-collaborative problem-solving in a pure zero-shot setting
SPP is the first zero-shot prompting method to enhance both knowledge and reasoning abilities in GPT-4 via emergent cognitive synergy
Discovery that cognitive synergy is an emergent property in GPT-4 and does not appear in less capable models, analogous to developmental stages in humans
In-depth analysis showing the necessity and effectiveness of dynamic, fine-grained personas over fixed, coarse-grained personas for complex task-solving

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Framework for learning planning-based reasoning via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on collected trajectories","Synthesizing process rewards from outcome supervision using offline simulation and partial trajectory exploration","Collecting seed trajectories from LLMs, sampling intermediate states, and estimating expected returns by retrying completions from those states","Training a Process Reward Model (PRM) using synthesized process rewards to annotate contrastive trajectory pairs for DPO"],"problems":["Reducing hallucination and flaws in LLM-generated reasoning processes","Improving reliability and faithfulness of LLM-generated rationales","Reducing high latency and cost in planning-based reasoning due to online assessment of intermediate states","Reducing reliance on costly and unscalable human process supervision for LLM training"],"datasets":["Logical reasoning benchmark (not named explicitly)","Mathematical reasoning benchmark (not named explicitly)"],"metrics":["Accuracy (implied by 'correct outcome')","Quality and conciseness of generated rationales (qualitative analysis)"],"results":[{"metric":"Model performance (7B model) vs GPT-3.5-Turbo","value":"7B model surpasses GPT-3.5-Turbo on challenging logical reasoning benchmarks"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel framework for synthesizing process rewards from outcome annotations using offline simulation and trajectory collection to induce planning-based reasoning","Reduces reliance on human annotation by using outcome supervision to backpropagate rewards to intermediate states","Demonstrates that the method improves both the quality and conciseness of generated rationales compared to strong baselines","Shows that the approach is more efficient than search-based inference by avoiding frequent online assessments of intermediate states"]}

methods: Framework for learning planning-based reasoning via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on collected trajectories
Synthesizing process rewards from outcome supervision using offline simulation and partial trajectory exploration
Collecting seed trajectories from LLMs, sampling intermediate states, and estimating expected returns by retrying completions from those states
Training a Process Reward Model (PRM) using synthesized process rewards to annotate contrastive trajectory pairs for DPO
problems: Reducing hallucination and flaws in LLM-generated reasoning processes
Improving reliability and faithfulness of LLM-generated rationales
Reducing high latency and cost in planning-based reasoning due to online assessment of intermediate states
Reducing reliance on costly and unscalable human process supervision for LLM training
metrics: Quality and conciseness of generated rationales (qualitative analysis)

results.value: 7B model surpasses GPT-3.5-Turbo on challenging logical reasoning benchmarks

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel framework for synthesizing process rewards from outcome annotations using offline simulation and trajectory collection to induce planning-based reasoning
Reduces reliance on human annotation by using outcome supervision to backpropagate rewards to intermediate states
Demonstrates that the method improves both the quality and conciseness of generated rationales compared to strong baselines
Shows that the approach is more efficient than search-based inference by avoiding frequent online assessments of intermediate states

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Self-Refine: an iterative self-refinement algorithm using a single LLM for generation, feedback, and refinement without additional training or supervision","Few-shot prompting to guide the LLM in generating feedback and incorporating it into improved drafts"],"problems":["Improving the quality of outputs generated by large language models (LLMs) without additional training or supervision","Addressing the limitations of LLMs in tasks with multifaceted objectives or hard-to-define goals, such as dialogue response generation and code readability enhancement","Providing a general refinement approach that does not require domain-specific data, external supervision, or reward models"],"datasets":["Seven diverse tasks spanning natural language and source-code generation (exact dataset names not specified in the provided content)"],"metrics":["Human preference","Automatic metrics (not specified in detail)","Task performance (percentage improvement)"],"results":[{"metric":"Average task performance improvement","value":"~20% absolute improvement over one-step generation"},{"metric":"Task performance improvement on GPT-3.5 and GPT-4","value":"5% absolute improvement"},{"metric":"Code generation improvement (Codex/code-davinci-002)","value":"up to 13% absolute improvement"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Self-Refine, a simple, standalone, and general iterative self-refinement approach that uses a single LLM for generation, feedback, and refinement, requiring no additional training, supervision, or reward models","Demonstration that even state-of-the-art LLMs like GPT-4 can be further improved at test time using iterative self-feedback and refinement","Applicability of Self-Refine across diverse tasks and domains without the need for domain-specific data or human assistance"]}

methods: Self-Refine: an iterative self-refinement algorithm using a single LLM for generation, feedback, and refinement without additional training or supervision

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["ReST-MCTS*: a reinforced self-training approach for LLMs integrating process reward guidance with a modified Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS*)","Automatic per-step process reward annotation via MCTS* rollouts","Training of policy and reward models using high-quality reasoning traces selected by tree search"],"problems":["Low-quality fine-tuning datasets in LLM self-training due to incorrect intermediate reasoning steps","Dependence on dense human annotation for per-step process rewards in LLM self-training","Difficulty in automatically acquiring high-quality reasoning traces and reliable reward signals for LLM self-training"],"datasets":["SciBench","MATH"],"metrics":["Accuracy (on reasoning tasks)"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy (MCTS* vs. Self-Consistency and Best-of-N on SciBench and MATH)","value":"MCTS* achieves higher accuracy than Self-Consistency and Best-of-N under the same search budget (see Figure 2)"},{"metric":"Process reward model quality (ReST-MCTS* vs. MATH-SHEPHERD)","value":"ReST-MCTS* leads to a higher-quality process reward model (see Table 3)"},{"metric":"Self-training performance (ReST-MCTS* vs. ReST^EM and Self-Rewarding)","value":"ReST-MCTS* outperforms ReST^EM and Self-Rewarding on multiple reasoning benchmarks (see Table 2 and Table 4)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes ReST-MCTS*, a self-training approach that automatically generates per-step process rewards using MCTS* without requiring human annotation.","Introduces a method to annotate process rewards for each intermediate node via sufficient rollouts, enabling high-quality trace selection.","Demonstrates that the tree-search policy (MCTS*) achieves higher accuracy than prior LLM reasoning baselines (Best-of-N, Tree-of-Thought) under the same search budget.","Shows that the reward generator in ReST-MCTS* produces a higher-quality process reward model compared to previous techniques."]}

methods: ReST-MCTS*: a reinforced self-training approach for LLMs integrating process reward guidance with a modified Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS*)
Automatic per-step process reward annotation via MCTS* rollouts
Training of policy and reward models using high-quality reasoning traces selected by tree search
problems: Low-quality fine-tuning datasets in LLM self-training due to incorrect intermediate reasoning steps
Dependence on dense human annotation for per-step process rewards in LLM self-training
Difficulty in automatically acquiring high-quality reasoning traces and reliable reward signals for LLM self-training

results.metric: Accuracy (MCTS* vs. Self-Consistency and Best-of-N on SciBench and MATH)
results.value: MCTS* achieves higher accuracy than Self-Consistency and Best-of-N under the same search budget (see Figure 2)

results.metric: Process reward model quality (ReST-MCTS* vs. MATH-SHEPHERD)
results.value: ReST-MCTS* leads to a higher-quality process reward model (see Table 3)

results.metric: Self-training performance (ReST-MCTS* vs. ReST^EM and Self-Rewarding)
results.value: ReST-MCTS* outperforms ReST^EM and Self-Rewarding on multiple reasoning benchmarks (see Table 2 and Table 4)

novelty_claims: Proposes ReST-MCTS*, a self-training approach that automatically generates per-step process rewards using MCTS* without requiring human annotation.
Introduces a method to annotate process rewards for each intermediate node via sufficient rollouts, enabling high-quality trace selection.
Demonstrates that the tree-search policy (MCTS*) achieves higher accuracy than prior LLM reasoning baselines (Best-of-N, Tree-of-Thought) under the same search budget.
Shows that the reward generator in ReST-MCTS* produces a higher-quality process reward model compared to previous techniques.

methods: ReST^EM (Reinforced Self-Training with Expectation-Maximization): a self-training method where the model generates samples, filters them using binary feedback, fine-tunes on these samples, and repeats the process
Expectation-Maximization framework for reinforcement learning applied to self-training of language models
Ablation studies to analyze the effect of number of model-generated solutions, training problems, and iterations
problems: Limited performance of language models due to scarcity and cost of high-quality human-generated data
Scalability of self-training methods for large language models in problem-solving domains (math and code generation)
Effectiveness of model-generated synthetic data compared to human-generated data for fine-tuning LLMs
novelty_claims: Introduction of ReST^EM, a principled expectation-maximization approach for self-training large language models using model-generated data and scalar feedback
Demonstration that self-training with model-generated solutions can surpass training on human-generated solutions in complex problem-solving domains such as mathematics and code generation
Empirical evidence that ReST^EM scales favorably with model size and reduces dependence on human-generated data
Comprehensive ablation studies identifying key factors for optimal self-training performance
Demonstration of robust transfer capabilities of ReST^EM-fine-tuned models to held-out benchmarks

methods: Multiagent debate among multiple language model instances, where each model proposes, critiques, and updates answers over several rounds to reach consensus

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Chain of thought prompting (providing few-shot exemplars with intermediate reasoning steps in prompts)"],"problems":["Improving reasoning abilities (arithmetic, commonsense, symbolic reasoning) in large language models","Overcoming limitations of standard few-shot prompting and rationale-augmented finetuning for reasoning tasks"],"datasets":["GSM8K (math word problems)"],"metrics":["Solve rate (%)","Accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Solve rate (%) on GSM8K (PaLM 540B, chain-of-thought prompting)","value":"58%"},{"metric":"Solve rate (%) on GSM8K (PaLM 540B, standard prompting)","value":"18%"},{"metric":"Solve rate (%) on GSM8K (Finetuned GPT-3 175B)","value":"55%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes chain of thought prompting, a simple prompting method that elicits intermediate reasoning steps in large language models without finetuning.","Demonstrates that chain of thought prompting enables large language models to achieve state-of-the-art performance on complex reasoning tasks, surpassing prior methods including finetuned models.","Shows that reasoning abilities can emerge in sufficiently large language models via in-context learning with chain of thought exemplars, without the need for large rationale-augmented training datasets."]}

problems: Improving reasoning abilities (arithmetic, commonsense, symbolic reasoning) in large language models

novelty_claims: Proposes chain of thought prompting, a simple prompting method that elicits intermediate reasoning steps in large language models without finetuning.
Demonstrates that chain of thought prompting enables large language models to achieve state-of-the-art performance on complex reasoning tasks, surpassing prior methods including finetuned models.

Shows that reasoning abilities can emerge in sufficiently large language models via in-context learning with chain of thought exemplars, without the need for large rationale-augmented training datasets.

novelty_claims: Propose a preference strength measurement metric based on multi-reward model voting to identify and mitigate incorrect and ambiguous preferences in RLHF datasets.

Apply contrastive learning and meta-learning to reward modeling for better generalization to out-of-distribution data and iterative RLHF.
Demonstrate that these methods lead to more stable RL processes and significant improvements in final alignment performance.

methods: AGENTVERSE: a general multi-agent framework inspired by human group problem-solving, featuring dynamic adjustment of agent group composition based on task progress
Splitting problem-solving into four stages: Expert Recruitment, Collaborative Decision-Making, Action Execution, and Evaluation with feedback loops
problems: Enabling effective collaboration among multiple autonomous agents for complex real-world tasks
Overcoming the limitations of static agent roles and rigid collaboration in previous multi-agent systems
Exploring and leveraging emergent social behaviors in multi-agent collaboration
datasets: Embodied AI tasks (including Minecraft game playing)

results.value: Multi-agent groups deployed by AGENTVERSE outperform single agents across diverse tasks
Observed volunteer, conformity, and destructive behaviors in collaborative settings

novelty_claims: Proposes AGENTVERSE, a general and dynamic multi-agent framework inspired by human group dynamics, allowing for adaptive group composition during problem-solving
Demonstrates the effectiveness of AGENTVERSE across a wide range of tasks (text, reasoning, coding, tool use, embodied AI), showing improved performance over single-agent systems
Systematically explores and discusses emergent social behaviors (volunteerism, conformity, destructive actions) in multi-agent collaboration, and strategies to leverage or mitigate them
